currentUse: Active Roman Catholic parish church (Diocese of Grand Rapids) with weekly Masses — but designated in the diocese's June 2025 pastoral plan for merger into the Basilica of St. Adalbert and eventual sale of the property upon the current pastor's retirement
summary: SS. Peter and Paul Parish at 520 Myrtle Street NW, Grand Rapids, remains a standing, functioning Catholic parish as of mid-2026: its website posts weekly bulletins through July 19, 2026, lists a full Mass schedule (Sunday 9:30 a.m., Saturday 4:00 p.m. vigil, daily Masses Tuesday–Saturday), and names Father Mark Bauer as pastor, with a parish office, school facility, and its own cemetery on Walker Avenue NW. However, the parish's long-term survival is now formally in question: the Diocese of Grand Rapids' "Rooted in Christ" pastoral plan, promulgated June 29, 2025 amid a priest shortage (one ordination in 2024, none in 2025; 21 parishes merging, 14 clustering), directs that "upon the retirement of the pastor, the parish of Ss. Peter and Paul in Grand Rapids will merge with the Basilica of St. Adalbert," and that the church "will continue to be used for religious purposes until the merger is complete and the property is sold." The plan's own consultation appendix records parishioner resistance — "No one affirmed the current proposed plan" — with the parish asking the diocese to re-evaluate at the time of the retirement rather than commit now to merger and sale, citing its neighborhood outreach (food pantry partnerships, utility/rental assistance, a women's transitional home in the former convent) and its presence on the West Side "for over 120 years." Crain's Grand Rapids Business covered the plan on July 17, 2025 under the headline that the diocese would close four churches and free their sites for reuse, featuring a photo of SS. Peter and Paul. The surrounding Lithuanian heritage ecosystem described in the archive record still exists: the Global True Lithuania encyclopedia describes the 1920s-built church as operating alongside three century-old Lithuanian clubs and two Lithuanian cemeteries, a combination it calls unique among American cities of comparable size. No merger date, sale price, or buyer exists yet, because the trigger — the pastor's retirement — has not occurred.

developments.headline: Diocesan 'Rooted in Christ' plan slates SS. Peter and Paul for merger and eventual property sale
developments.detail: The Diocese of Grand Rapids promulgated its Rooted in Christ pastoral plan on June 29, 2025 (the Solemnity of Saints Peter and Paul), responding to a priest shortage: one priestly ordination in 2024, none in 2025, and seven pastors lost to senior status or reassignment in 2025. The plan orders that upon the retirement of the pastor, SS. Peter and Paul in Grand Rapids will merge with the Basilica of St. Adalbert; the church 'will continue to be used for religious purposes until the merger is complete and the property is sold.' Diocese-wide, 21 parishes merge and 14 form clusters.

sources.title: Rooted in Christ Pastoral Planning Process (final plan, pp. 15-16)
Crippling priest shortage leads to restructuring of Grand Rapids Diocese

developments.headline: Parishioners formally rejected the merger-and-sale proposal during consultation
developments.detail: The plan's own consultation appendix records SS. Peter and Paul's feedback: 'No one affirmed the current proposed plan.' The parish asked instead that the diocese evaluate merger vs. cluster only when the current canonical administrator/pastor announces retirement, weighing attendance, finances, and 'the impact of the closure on the westside neighborhood.' The parish documented active ministries it intends to continue — Northwest Food Pantry holiday food baskets, food vouchers, utility/rental assistance, Toys for Tots distribution, a women's transitional home in the former convent — noting it has served the neighborhood 'for over 120 years.'

sources.title: Rooted in Christ Pastoral Planning Process (parish feedback appendix)

developments.headline: Local business press covers the plan as freeing church sites for redevelopment
developments.detail: Crain's Grand Rapids Business reported that the Catholic diocese plans to close four West Michigan churches, 'opening land for development,' and illustrated the story with a photo of Saints Peter and Paul Catholic Church at 520 Myrtle Street on Grand Rapids' west side. (Article body is paywalled; the full list of four churches was not directly readable.)

sources.title: Catholic diocese to close 4 churches, freeing sites for reuse

developments.headline: Parish remains fully active one year after the plan: weekly bulletins, full Mass schedule, resident pastor
developments.detail: As of mid-July 2026 the parish website posts bulletins through July 19, 2026 and lists Masses Sunday 9:30 a.m., Tuesday 5:30 p.m. (with Perpetual Help novena), Wednesday-Saturday 8:00 a.m., and a Saturday 4:00 p.m. vigil. Father Mark Bauer is pastor; staff include a parish secretary, business/facilities manager, faith formation director, and music director. The parish operates its own SS. Peter & Paul Cemetery at 2000 Walker Ave. NW. The merger trigger — the pastor's retirement — has not occurred.

conflictsWithArchiveRecord: The archive record (status: standing, ending: standing, "survived") is confirmed as of mid-2026 — the parish is open, active, and diocese-owned, and the Global True Lithuania description of the neighborhood (operating church, three century-old Lithuanian clubs, two Lithuanian cemeteries, unique among American cities of that size) matches the archive's 2024 "best-preserved living first-wave Lithuanian neighborhood" characterization. However, there is a major refinement the archive does not capture: on June 29, 2025 the diocese's Rooted in Christ pastoral plan formally scheduled the parish's end — merger into the Basilica of St. Adalbert upon the current pastor's retirement, with the church used for worship only "until the merger is complete and the property is sold." The "survivor" status is therefore now time-limited and contingent, not open-ended. No contradiction on founding (parish feedback in the plan itself cites serving the neighborhood "for over 120 years," consistent with 1904) or ownership (diocese_rc).
gaps: No merger or closure date exists — the trigger is the pastor's (Fr. Mark Bauer's) retirement, which had not occurred or been announced as of the July 2026 bulletins; therefore no sale price, buyer, or listing exists to report. The Crain's Grand Rapids article body (July 17, 2025, "diocese to close 4 churches") is paywalled, so the full list of four churches slated for sale was not directly read — SS. Peter and Paul's inclusion was verified instead from the plan PDF itself. The diocese's own parish directory page (grdiocese.org) returned HTTP 403 and could not be read, and the parish website's TLS certificate is expired (pages were retrieved despite it). Searches found no press coverage specifically of the Lithuanian community's or preservationists' response beyond the parish's own feedback recorded in the plan appendix, and no coverage of a 2024 120th-anniversary event. The parish website's Upcoming Events page is empty. Whether the parish today still holds any Lithuanian-language services or explicitly Lithuanian programming could not be verified from opened sources (the website shows no Lithuanian-language content).
